text: The surgeon uses a robotic needle driver to place stitches, leaving them loose at the end, while pointing the needle driver at the hole for the bedside assistant to remove it.
The suction cannula is inserted through the port and directed towards the hole left in the tissue flap.
The assistant approaches with the suction cannula, either with a finger covering the opening or not, demonstrating a more dramatic effect when the finger remains on the cannula.

The surgeon observes the lateral edge of the mesh, which appears slightly curled, noting that in previous cases, the curl was more pronounced despite dissection extending at least a centimeter beyond the mesh edge.
The surgeon presses down on the mesh to flatten it, minimizing any lift and ensuring it lies flat against the underlying tissue.

The surgeon identifies a significant lift at the edge of the flap and attempts to push it down. When unable to flatten it, a hole is created near the area, followed by dissection to ensure the mesh sits properly. The hole is then closed using a 3-0 suture.
After the cannula is removed, the surgical pocket appears well desufflated, although occasionally it may not maintain this state.
The surgeon runs the V-lock suture back two throws before cutting the suture to complete the procedure.
The surgeon demonstrates the rapid desufflation of a bilateral angular hernia pocket through a large-bore cannula, highlighting the difficulty of needle insertion in a thick abdominal wall.

The surgeon observes the mesh slightly lifted up and proceeds to reposition it. If the mesh is significantly elevated, the assistant may remove the cannula to allow CO2 to re-enter the pocket for readjustment.
The surgeon removes the cannula and pulls the suture tight, observing the peritoneum which appears heavy due to the bladder. Occasionally, CO-II re-enters through a small hole.

The surgeon completes the procedure, ensuring no risk of injuring the inferior gastric vessels. The fourth robotic arm is utilized due to the scrotal hernia, allowing for reconfiguration of the tissue flap to address tissue loss and verify that the surgical site is watertight or airtight.
The surgeon utilizes the fourth arm positioned in the right lower quadrant to leave the needle driver in place, facilitating the rapid evacuation of the pocket.
The surgeon visually inspects the placement of the mesh to ensure it provides complete coverage around the scrotal hernia repair site.
